1:
Chronicles opens on an arresting word its parallel does not use, and the witnesses fasten at once on the chapter's hardest knot: "Satan stood up against Israel, and provoked David to number Israel," where 2 Samuel 24:1 ascribes the same impulse to "the anger of the LORD." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that "Satan" here lacks the article, and would render it "an adversary, a hostile influence, arose against Israel," as the sons of Zeruiah are called "a Satan" to David in 2 Samuel 19:23 and as the word is used in 1 Kings 11:14; when the great enemy of mankind is meant the word takes the article, as in Zechariah 3:1. He then identifies this adversary with the wrath of God itself, "that aspect of the Divine nature under which it pursues to destruction whatever is opposed to its own perfection" (quoting Delitzsch); the wrath could urge David only because thoughts of pride and self-sufficiency had intruded into a heart once humbly reliant on its Maker, and God did not interpose but let the corrupt motive work out its own penal results, as in Romans 1:24. He even suggests the older reading in Samuel may have been "an adversary incited David," the word Satan having dropped from the text.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry traces the foul stream to its foundation. That Satan, the sworn adversary of God's Israel, should stand up to weaken, diminish, and eclipse its glory is no wonder; the marvel is that he should influence David, the man after God's own heart, so that no saint may think himself out of reach of temptation till he comes to heaven. Henry reconciles the two texts as Ellicott does: God "moved" David only in that, for wise and holy ends, he permitted the devil to do it, for God is not the author of sin and tempts no man. He draws the sting of it too: the devil does us more mischief by tempting us to sin than by accusing us, and his greatest spite against the church is to tempt its rulers to pride.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads Satan's "standing" as the accuser's posture before a tribunal, standing before the Lord to accuse David and Israel and to beg leave to tempt him, Scripture speaking of divine things after the manner of men. Adam Clarke simply refers the reader to the parallel at 2 Samuel 24:1, while Keil and Delitzsch place the census in the last years of the reign and observe that the two records, drawn from a common source, diverge markedly in their conception of the same event.
2:
"And David said to Joab and to the rulers of the people, Go, number Israel from Beersheba even to Dan." Both witnesses here are grammatical-historical, and they read the command for what its very wording betrays. Keil and Delitzsch observe that the naming of the "rulers of the people" beside Joab fits the facts, for 2 Samuel 24:4 shows Joab did not carry out the census alone but was assisted by the captains of the host; and the Chronicler's phrasing, "bring the number of them to me, that I may know it," follows the idiom of Deuteronomy 1:11 rather than the doubled expression peculiar to the author of Samuel, as at 2 Samuel 12:8.

Charles Ellicott presses the point that this staff of "captains of the people," omitted in Samuel (which reads only "Joab the captain of the host, who was with him"), was nonetheless associated with the work of the census there; and the fact that Joab and his officers were deputed to it "seems to prove that it was of a military character." He notes a chain of different verbs: the "number" of this verse is siphru, to enrol or register, distinct from the manah of v.1 and from Samuel's shut, "run over" — the very word that, in the prologue of Job 1:7, Satan uses of his own wanderings to and fro over the earth, a quiet verbal echo of the tempter of the opening verse. And "from Beersheba even to Dan" runs south to north, the reverse of the usual order in Judges 20:1 and 1 Samuel 3:20.

The two voices do not divide; together they let the grammar disclose the heart of the matter. A king who deputes his generals to count only the men who draw sword is reviewing his fighting strength, and it is precisely there — in the martial reckoning of a realm at peace — that the pride and self-sufficiency the witnesses named at the first verse finds its foothold. What looks like sober administration is, in the sight of God, the arm of flesh being measured and admired. The very multiplicity of verbs Ellicott traces — to number, to enrol, to run over — betrays how closely the historians dwelt on the act itself, as though the counting of the drawn swords were the very substance of the crime.
3:
Joab's protest is the moral center of the census, and the witnesses gather to weigh it. "The LORD make his people an hundred times so many more as they be: but, my lord the king, are they not all my lord's servants? why then doth my lord require this thing? why will he be a cause of trespass to Israel?" The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott shows how the Chronicler has recast the older words: "answered" is simply "said"; the blessing is an abridged form of Samuel's; where Chronicles has "are they not all my lord's servants," Samuel records the different wish "that the eyes of my lord the king may see it"; and the closing clause, "why will he be a cause of trespass to Israel?" is the Chronicler's own explanatory addition, absent from Samuel. Keil and Delitzsch read Joab's opening question as a loyal disclaimer: by "are they not all my lord's servants," Joab allays any suspicion that he grudged the king the joy of reigning over a great and numerous people. His reluctance is not disloyalty but a truer wisdom.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had already made much of that reluctance. Joab, forward in anything that served the king's honour, here put in his remonstrance before he would begin, on two grounds: it was a needless thing, for God had promised to multiply them and there was no cause to question the promise or the people's loyalty; and it was a dangerous thing, by which he might become a cause of trespass to Israel and provoke God against them. Henry marks the irony that "the most learned in the laws of God are not always the most quick-sighted in the application of those laws" — Joab, the rough soldier, apprehended the danger that David, the man of God's own heart, did not.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley takes up that very phrase, "a cause of trespass," and turns it into a rule of providence: God commonly punishes a people for the sins of their rulers, because they are for the most part guilty of those sins in one kind or another, or at least because God takes the occasion to reckon with them for all their sins at once. So the witnesses converge on the tragedy of the scene: the servant sees what the king cannot, warns him plainly, and is overruled — and a whole nation will bleed for a pride it did not devise but will not escape.
4:
"Nevertheless the king's word prevailed against Joab. Wherefore Joab departed, and went throughout all Israel, and came to Jerusalem." Only the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott lingers here, and he fills the bare notice with the geography and the calendar that Chronicles omits. "Departed" is, in Samuel, "went out," that is, from the king's presence; and the Chronicler passes over the long route described in 2 Samuel 24:4-8, where Joab and his party crossed Jordan to Aroer, Jazer, Gilead, and Dan, then rounded to Zidon and the fortress of Tyre and all the cities of the Hivite and Canaanite, and came out at the Negeb of Judah to Beersheba.

The two details Ellicott draws from that omitted itinerary carry real weight. The business, he notes, "occupied nine months and twenty days"; and the very fact that the commander-in-chief of David's forces and his chief officers could find leisure for so long an undertaking "indicates a time of settled peace." From this he dates the census to the later years of the reign — not a wartime muster forced by necessity, but the deliberate project of a kingdom at rest. That the Chronicler omits the route altogether, Ellicott observes, is of a piece with his method throughout, hastening past the mere machinery of the census toward the sin and its remedy.

That dating is more than antiquarian. It sharpens the charge the earlier witnesses laid against the sin. This was no anxious counting of soldiers on the eve of battle, when a king might be pardoned for taking stock of his strength; it was the leisured self-congratulation of a realm with no enemy at its gates, nine months and twenty days spent tallying the arm of flesh for its own sake. The "needless thing" Joab had named at v.3 is here confirmed by the very ease and length of its execution. The king's word prevailed against his best counsellor, and the wheels of a great administration turned for the better part of a year to accomplish a work whose only fruit would be a plague. Ellicott stands alone on this verse, but the note he sounds is the chapter's: that David's ruin sprang not from the pressure of events but from the ease of his own heart.
5:
The muster totals are reported, and with them the first open divergence between the two histories. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke sets the figures side by side: here "all they of Israel were a thousand thousand and an hundred thousand," with Judah four hundred and seventy thousand, whereas in 2 Samuel 24:9 the men of Israel are reckoned eight hundred thousand and the men of Judah five hundred thousand.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott takes up the discrepancy at length. "Number" here is miphqad, a muster or census, and Chronicles substitutes "David" for Samuel's "the king." Of the differing totals he offers three possibilities. Judah's five hundred thousand in Samuel may be a round number, our four hundred and seventy thousand the more exact reckoning. Israel's eleven hundred thousand may be a simple error of transcription; or, since these numbers "were not registered in the royal archives," as 1 Chronicles 27:24 expressly states, the traditions may honestly have varied; or, most ingeniously, our larger figure may include David's standing army, reckoned in 1 Chronicles 27:1 at some two hundred and eighty-eight thousand, in round numbers three hundred thousand — so that Samuel's eight hundred thousand plus that standing force yields the Chronicler's eleven hundred thousand.

So the debate is genuine, and Ellicott does not force a single answer. Whether the gap between the accounts is scribal, traditional, or a matter of what each writer chose to count, the numbers will not simply be harmonized by arithmetic. Yet the witnesses agree on what the numbers are for. This is the proud tally David had demanded, "that I may know it"; the great sum of drawn swords that flattered the king's heart. The very grandeur of the figures — more than a million and a half men of war — is the measure of the temptation, and it will shortly become the measure of the loss, when the God who could be counted on to multiply his people takes it upon himself to number them another way. The register of David's strength stands here at its proud height, on the page before the plague.
6:
"But Levi and Benjamin counted he not among them: for the king's word was abominable to Joab." Why these two tribes were spared draws three distinct explanations, and the witnesses divide over them. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reports the rabbins: Joab, foreseeing that the census would bring destruction, purposed to save two tribes, and had his answers ready — were he asked why he left out Levi, he would say the Levites are not reckoned among the children of Israel; were he asked about Benjamin, that Benjamin had already been all but destroyed for the outrage at Gibeah, recorded in Judges 21:6, and if punished again by numbering, who of them would remain?

Wesley grants that reason a place but sets a deeper one above it: chiefly it was by God's gracious providence to Levi, as devoted to his service, and to Benjamin, the least of the tribes, once almost extinct and foreseen to be faithful to the house of David at the coming division of the kingdom, so that God would not have them diminished. Joab, for his part, had specious pretexts — Levi were no warriors, and the king's command reached only those that drew sword; Benjamin, small and bordering Jerusalem, might easily be numbered afterward.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott takes a soberer line. The verse is wanting in Samuel but probably stood in the original source, its style bearing no mark of a later hand. The sacerdotal tribe of Levi would naturally be exempt from a census taken for military purposes, as at Numbers 1:47; and 1 Chronicles 27:24 expressly asserts the census was never completed — a result with which Joab's disapproval had much to do. Josephus speaks as if Joab simply had not had time to include Benjamin; and he may have feared to give offence to the tribe of Saul. So the witnesses genuinely diverge: was the omission Joab's deliberate contrivance to spare a remnant, God's own providential shielding of two tribes, or merely the ragged edge of a census abandoned before it was done? Each reading honours a different actor — the shrewd general, the merciful God, the unfinished record — and the text does not compel a choice.
7:
"And God was displeased with this thing; therefore he smote Israel." The verse turns the corner from sin to stroke, and the witnesses read it as both a verdict and a heading. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry sets David under the rod — "that rod of correction which drives out the foolishness that is bound up in the heart, the foolishness of pride." God takes notice of, and is displeased with, the sins of his people, and no sin is more displeasing to him than pride of heart; nor is anything more humbling and mortifying to a gracious soul than to see itself under God's displeasure. If God's dearest children do amiss, Henry insists, they must expect to smart for it.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott observes that this verse too is absent from Samuel, which reads instead, "and David's heart smote him after he had numbered the people"; the Chronicler's sentence functions rather as "a sort of general heading, or anticipative summary" of the narrative that follows, its construction the unusual one the margin rightly renders. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley states the ground of the displeasure most bluntly: the thing was done "without any colour of necessity, and out of mere curiosity, and ostentation."

Here the witnesses press the question the whole census has raised — precisely what made the numbering sinful — and they answer it from different angles. For Henry, and for the grammatical-historical reading of the opening verses, the root was pride of heart, a king's self-reliance on his own strength intruding upon a trust once fixed on God alone. For Wesley, the sin lay in its sheer needlessness, an act of curiosity and vainglory with no warrant of necessity behind it. The two are not finally at odds; the ostentation Wesley names is only pride's outward face, and the self-sufficiency the others name is its inward spring. But the emphasis genuinely differs, and the difference matters, for it locates the offence either in the heart's hidden posture or in the deed's manifest vanity. What none of them doubts is that God was displeased with a thing the world thought harmless, and that his displeasure, once kindled, does not spend itself in words but issues at once in a smiting the rest of the chapter will unfold.
8:
David's confession breaks in with the chapter's most naked words: "I have sinned greatly, because I have done this thing: but now, I beseech thee, do away the iniquity of thy servant; for I have done very foolishly." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that the verse is verbatim with its parallel, save for the Chronicler's characteristic substitution of "God" for "Jehovah," the added phrase "this thing" in the first half, and the omission of the Divine Name in the second. "Do away," he explains, is literally "cause to pass over," and so away; and he fixes the moment from the fuller account of 2 Samuel 24:10-11, where David's conscience misgave him in the night, before ever the prophet Gad came to him.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry dwells on the quality of that repentance. David, he says, made a very penitent confession of his sin and prayed earnestly for the pardon of it: now he owned that he had sinned, had sinned greatly, had done foolishly, very foolishly; and he entreated that, however he might be corrected for it, the iniquity of it might be done away. There is no excusing here, no shifting of the blame that a moment before might have fallen on Satan or on Joab or on the anger of the LORD; the king takes the whole weight of it on himself.

The witnesses stand together, and the very brevity of the material lets its force be felt. The greatness of the sin is matched, word for word, by the depth of the confession: "sinned greatly," "done very foolishly." Where the world had seen no evil in a census, David now sees folly and iniquity, and names them as his own. It is the mark, Henry's reading implies, of a gracious soul under the rod — not to dispute the stroke but to confess the fault, and to seek not first the removal of the punishment but the doing away of the guilt. The prayer that the iniquity be "caused to pass over" looks past the plague to the sin beneath it; and it prepares the fuller intercession of v.17, where the same penitent king will offer his own head in the place of his people.
9:
"And the LORD spake unto Gad, David's seer, saying" — and the whole scene turns upon that word given to a prophet. Only the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott comments here, but he draws several threads together. The order in Samuel, he notes, is fuller and looks more original: "And David arose in the morning; now a word of Jehovah had come to Gad the prophet, a seer of David, saying." The title "David's seer" he would render "a seer of David's," the very phrase applied to Heman in 1 Chronicles 25:5. For Gad himself he points back to 1 Samuel 22:5, where the prophet first attends the fugitive David, and to 1 Chronicles 29:29; and from that last passage, he observes, it has been inferred that it was Gad who wrote the original record of the census.

The single voice, closely read, opens the theology of the verse. The judgment does not fall as blind calamity; it comes announced, through the appointed channel of a prophet's word. The seer who had stood by David in the cave and the wilderness of his exile, in the days recorded at 1 Samuel 22:5, now stands by him in the graver peril of his sin — the same man of God attending the king from his first troubles to his last. And if, as Ellicott allows the inference from 1 Chronicles 29:29, Gad himself set down this very history, then the chapter comes to us as something near an eyewitness memoir, the prophet recording the word he was given to carry.

There is mercy hidden in the mere fact of the message. That God speaks at all — that he sends Gad rather than simply striking — means the door is not yet shut. The word that here brings the offer of chastisement is the same office that will presently bring, in v.18, the command to build the altar of reconciliation. The seer is God's instrument for both the sentence and its remedy, and the verse that names him quietly assures the reader that a God who still speaks to his erring servant has not yet given him over.
10:
God, through Gad, lays a choice before the king: "I offer thee three things: choose thee one of them, that I may do it unto thee." Only the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott comments, and his interest is in the exact words the Chronicler has chosen. The verse nearly coincides with its parallel, he says, but the small variations "look like corrections and explanatory or paraphrastic substitutions." The word "go" is here the plain imperative rather than the less usual infinitive of Samuel; "saying" is added for clearness; and, most tellingly, the easier phrase "I offer thee" — literally, I spread or lay before thee — is given in place of Samuel's curious "I lift up," that is, impose, upon thee, a change (noteh for notel) such as is common in the Targum. Even the pronoun "of them," masculine in Samuel, stands more correctly in the feminine here.

That one grammatical adjustment which Ellicott flags carries more than the antiquarian could guess. Where the older text has God "impose" the three judgments upon David, the Chronicler has him "lay them before" the king, as one spreads out wares for another to weigh and choose. It is a striking condescension, and it is the whole strange mercy of the passage: the offended LORD does not simply inflict the penalty his justice demands but grants the offender a voice in his own chastening. The sinner who has provoked the stroke is invited to reach out his hand and take which stroke he will.

The change of verb thus preserves, in miniature, the theology the following verses will enlarge. God's dealing with David is judicial but not merely punitive; the rod is offered, not flung. And by leaving the choice to the king, God draws David's own conscience into the sentence, so that whatever falls, David must own that he chose it. The three things laid before him — which the next verse will name — are set out not as an ambush but as an open hand, and the grammar Ellicott recovers is the trace of that divine forbearance in the very syntax of the threat.
11:
Gad comes to David and repeats the divine offer: "Thus saith the LORD, Choose thee." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott, the lone voice, first settles the wording — Samuel's pleonastic "and told him, and said" is smoothed here, and "Choose thee" stands where Samuel put a direct question, "shall there come to thee seven years' famine?" The word rendered "choose," literally "take," he notes, is one of later Hebrew use, as the Syriac's qabbel confirms.

But Ellicott's most illuminating contribution is to set the three penalties against the background of the ancient Near East. He quotes a curse from the Annals of Tiglath-Pileser I, about 1120 B.C.: "May Assur and Anum, the great gods my lords, mightily rebuke him and curse him with grievous curse... The overthrow of his army may they work!... May Rimmon with evil pestilence his land cut off! Want of crops, famine, corpses, to his country may be cast!" Here, from a pagan king's own inscription, is the very triad now laid before David — the sword that overthrows the army, the pestilence, and the famine that fills a land with corpses.

The single witness, read closely, opens the meaning of the moment. These three — war, famine, plague — were the recognized scourges by which the ancient world knew that heaven was against a nation; the standing repertoire of covenant curse. What Tiglath-Pileser could only call down upon his enemies in the name of Assur and Anum, the living God now sets before his own anointed as the measured consequence of a numbered pride. The dreadfulness of the choice is real: each of the three is a public calamity, falling not on David alone but on the people he had counted. Yet even in naming them the LORD keeps the initiative wholly in his own hand — "that I may do it unto thee" — so that the penalties are not the blind operation of fate but the deliberate act of a God who both threatens and, as the sequel will show, restrains. Ellicott's very attention to the late-Hebrew word for "choose" quietly dates the Chronicler's telling, betraying a writer who looks back across the centuries at David's fatal choice.
12:
Now the three are spelled out — three years of famine, three months of flight before the sword, or three days of the pestilence — and at once a second numerical divergence from Samuel surfaces. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke marks it plainly: here it is "three days" of pestilence and, in the first option, three years of famine, whereas 2 Samuel 24:13 names seven years of famine.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott judges the Chronicler's "three years" to be correct, "as harmonising with the three months and three days" of the other two visitations; Samuel's "seven," he suggests, "perhaps originated in some scribe's memory of the famine described in Genesis 41:30" and following. He works through the rest of the verse: Chronicles' "to be destroyed" answers to Samuel's "thy flying," which is doubtless right; and the fuller clause about "the sword of the LORD... throughout all the coasts of Israel" appears to be an exegetical expansion of the older, briefer statement, though others hold the reverse. He notes that in the thought of those times pestilence and "the sword" or "angel of the LORD" would readily suggest each other, comparing the three judgments gathered in Ezekiel 14:21 and Leviticus 26:25. He renders the Chronicler's closing "now therefore advise thyself" as simply "And now see," where Samuel has "Now know and see" — the seer pressing the stricken king to a swift decision.

The debate, then, is whether the famine was to last three years or seven, and the witnesses lean the Chronicler's way while acknowledging the older reading. Ellicott's argument is one of symmetry: three days, three months, three years form a deliberate ascending series, and "seven" breaks the pattern, most likely by a copyist's mind sliding to Joseph's famine. Clarke simply records the difference without resolving it. What matters beneath the arithmetic is the shape of the choice — a swift, brief blow against a long, grinding one — and the note, carried by Ellicott from Ezekiel 14:21, that all three are equally the sword of the LORD, so that David is not choosing between God's hand and some other, but only which form of that one hand he will fall beneath.
13:
David's answer is the theological summit of the chapter: "I am in a great strait: let me fall now into the hand of the LORD; for very great are his mercies: but let me not fall into the hand of man." The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke unfolds the reasoning by way of the Targum, which has David weigh each option aloud — if he chose famine, men would say his own granaries were full and he cared not though Israel starved; if he chose war and flight, that he was a strong and warlike man who cared not though the people fell by the sword; therefore, brought into a great strait, "I will deliver myself now into the hand of the Word of the Lord, for his mercies are many; but into the hands of the children of men I will not deliver myself."

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott finds the verse almost identical with Samuel, noting that Chronicles' "Let me fall" refines Samuel's "Let us fall," and that David here confesses his inability to choose: only this much is clear to him, that it is better to be dependent on the compassion of God than of man. By implication he decides against the second alternative — flight before his enemies — and leaves the rest to God. Ellicott adds that all three were regarded as divine visitations, but the pestilence especially, "because of the apparent suddenness of its outbreak and the mysterious nature of its operation."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gathers the whole into a lesson on faith under the frown of God. David cast himself upon the mercy of God, though he knew that God was angry with him, and did not entertain any hard thoughts of him: "Let us fall into the hands of the LORD, for his mercies are great." Good men, Henry observes, even when God frowns upon them, think well of him — "Though he slay me, yet will I trust in him." So the witnesses stand as one on the pivot of the chapter: cornered, guilty, unable to choose, David does the one thing faith can do, and throws himself not out of God's hand but deeper into it, reckoning the mercy of the offended God safer than the tenderness of men.
14:
"So the LORD sent pestilence upon Israel: and there fell of Israel seventy thousand men." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that the opening clause stands as in Samuel while the rest is abridged; from the older account we learn what Chronicles omits, that "the plague raged throughout the land from dawn to the time of the evening sacrifice" — a single dreadful day, its bounds set by the hours of worship.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley and the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry press the terrible justice of the number. Wesley puts it in a sentence: David "was proud of the number of his people, but God took a course to make them fewer; justly is that we are proud of so taken from us, or embittered to us." Henry expands the same irony at length. David had heard of seventy thousand of his subjects struck dead in a few hours; he had been proud of the multitude, and divine Justice took a course to thin it. "David must have the people numbered," Henry writes; "Bring me the number of them, that I may know it. But now God numbers them after another manner, numbers to the sword"; and David had another reckoning of them brought him, "more to his confusion than to his satisfaction, namely, the number of the slain — a black bill of mortality, which is a drawback to his muster-roll."

The witnesses do not divide, and it is worth marking what they do not need to dispute: unlike the census totals of v.5 or the famine years of v.12, the figure of seventy thousand stands identical in both histories, a fixed and terrible fact around which the accounts agree. The point the witnesses drive home is the exact fitness of the punishment to the sin. The very thing in which David gloried — the sheer count of his fighting men — is the thing God diminishes, and the king who sent Joab out to swell his muster-roll receives instead a bill of the dead. The mercy David chose in v.13 is real, but it is mercy inside a judgment, and the judgment is measured to the very idol of the offence. Henry drives the point home in a sentence: "Justly is that taken from us, weakened, or embittered to us, which we are proud of."
15:
The destroying angel reaches Jerusalem and is stayed at the threshing-floor. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott works through the text with care: the reading of Samuel is probably right, "the angel stretched out his hand towards Jerusalem to destroy it," and the "God" of our verse is substituted for "Jehovah," itself a misreading of part of the Hebrew of Samuel. The clause "and as he was destroying, the LORD beheld," absent from Samuel, serves, in Bertheau's phrase, "to soften the harshness of the transition from the command to the countermand." It does not appear that Jerusalem was actually touched. The divine word he renders, by the Hebrew accents, "Enough now, stay (drop) thine hand." And of the name he notes that "Ornan" is spelt so throughout Chronicles, where Samuel has the less Hebrew-looking Araunah or Aravnah; such differences, he remarks, are natural in the spelling of foreign names, the Septuagint giving "Orna," the Syriac and Arabic "Aran."

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke brings the Targum's vivid amplification. The Word of the Lord sent the angel of death against Jerusalem to destroy it; "and he beheld the ashes of the binding of Isaac at the foot of the altar, and he remembered his covenant with Abraham... and the image of Jacob fixed on the throne of glory; and he turned in his Word from the evil which he designed to do unto them; and he said to the destroying angel, Cease." It is a legendary gloss, reported and not endorsed, but it fastens on the true hinge of the verse — that the arrest of judgment springs from remembered covenant mercy.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry states the plain sense the Targum dramatizes: a stop was put to the progress of the execution, for "when David repented of the sin God repented of the judgment," and ordered the destroying angel to stay his hand and sheath his sword. The witnesses agree on the wonder of the moment. At the very edge of the holy city, with the sword already drawn over it, the LORD beholds and relents; the countermand overtakes the command; and the place where the stroke is halted — a Jebusite's threshing-floor — is, though none yet says so, the ground on which the whole rest of the story, and a temple, will stand.
16:
"And David lifted up his eyes, and saw the angel of the LORD stand between the earth and the heaven, having a drawn sword in his hand stretched out over Jerusalem." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch observe that the Chronicle tells this whole episode more fully than 2 Samuel 24:17-25, which narrates only David's dealing with Araunah at length; in substance the two accounts perfectly correspond, but the Chronicle preserves several subordinate circumstances passed over in Samuel — here, the description of the angel with drawn sword stretched over the city. Charles Ellicott notes that this verse is not in Samuel, which yet records the essential fact that David "saw the angel that smote the people"; nothing in the style suggests a later hand. The "drawn sword in his hand" recalls the phrase of Numbers 22:23, and "stretched out" is the term used of the menace of divine wrath in Isaiah 5:25. That David and the elders, "covered with sackcloth," fell on their faces agrees, he adds, with Samuel's note that Araunah saw "the king and his servants" coming, and matches the postures of Joshua 5:14.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads the sackcloth simply: mourning garments, in which king and elders humbled themselves before God for their sins and deprecated his wrath against the people.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry dwells on the terror of the sight. It could not but be dreadful to David, a visible indication of the anger of heaven, threatening the utter destruction of that beloved city; the sight of an angel even on a friendly errand has made mighty men tremble, and how much more this, "an angel with a drawn sword in his hand, a flaming sword, like that of the cherubim, which turned every way to keep the way of the tree of life." While we lie under the wrath of God, Henry warns, the holy angels are armed against us, though we see them not as David did. The witnesses converge on a single scene of dread and penitence: heaven's warrior poised between earth and sky, and beneath him the guilty king, no longer proud of his thousands but prostrate in sackcloth among his elders, with nothing left to plead but mercy.
17:
David's intercession is the heart's cry of the whole chapter: "Is it not I that commanded the people to be numbered? even I it is that have sinned and done evil indeed; but as for these sheep, what have they done? let thine hand, I pray thee, O LORD my God, be on me, and on my father's house; but not on thy people, that they should be plagued." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch give the verse its plain force: David takes the entire guilt upon himself and his father's house, and pleads for the innocent flock. Charles Ellicott attends to the wording. The opening question, "Is it not I that commanded to number the people?" is wanting in Samuel and may be the Chronicler's addition for clearness, though it could be original; "even I it is that have sinned and done evil indeed" answers to Samuel's "Lo, I have sinned, and I have dealt crookedly." The phrase "but as for these sheep" he renders "but these, the sheep" — for, he observes, the king was the shepherd. The strange order of the closing words makes it likely, in his judgment, that two marginal glosses have crept into the text; they are not read in Samuel.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws out the penitent's whole posture. David accepted the punishment of his iniquity: "Let thy hand be on me, and on my father's house; I submit to the rod, only let me be the sufferer, for I am the sinner; mine is the guilty head at which the sword should be pointed." And he expressed a tender concern for the people, so that it went to his heart to see them plagued for his transgression — "These sheep, what have they done?"

The witnesses do not divide, and the figure they all seize on is the shepherd who would die for his sheep. Ellicott's philological note, that "the king was the shepherd," and Henry's pastoral reading meet in the same image: a guilty ruler who does not shelter behind his people but steps in front of them, offering his own head and his own house to the sword that hangs over the innocent. The pride that once counted the flock as its glory has become a love that would perish in the flock's place; and the chapter's darkest hour is also its most Christlike, the anointed king interceding, "on me be this iniquity," for the many who had done nothing.
18:
The judgment turns toward peace: "Then the angel of the LORD commanded Gad to say to David, that David should go up, and set up an altar unto the LORD in the threshingfloor of Ornan the Jebusite." The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads the reconciliation in every detail. The same angel that had, in God's name, carried on the war "is here forward to set on foot the treaty of peace; for angels do not desire the woeful day." The angel could have given the order to David himself, but chose to send it by his seer, "that he might put an honour upon the prophetic office" — as, Henry adds, the revelation of Jesus Christ was notified by the angel to John and by him to the churches, in Revelation 1:1. And the command to build an altar was itself "a blessed token of reconciliation."

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sharpens the tense: "Now the angel had told Gad to tell David." In Samuel the mediation of the angel is not mentioned; there "Gad came that day to David, and said, Go up." It is only in the later prophetical books of the canon, Ellicott observes, that angels are regularly introduced as the medium of communication between God and his prophets, as in Daniel 8:16 and Zechariah 1:9.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley echoes Henry almost word for word on the meaning of the command: "the commanding of David to build an altar was a blessed token of reconciliation; for if God had been pleased to kill him, he would not have commanded, because he would not have accepted, a sacrifice at his hands." The witnesses stand together on this bright turn. That God should not merely stay the sword but issue a positive command — build here, sacrifice here — is proof that his anger is spent and his favour returning. The very order to raise an altar is grace, for a God resolved on destruction does not appoint the means of atonement; and that the order comes through the prophet, not by a bare voice from heaven, honours the appointed channel by which God still speaks his peace to penitents.
19:
"And David went up at the saying of Gad, which he spake in the name of the LORD." The verse records nothing but obedience, and only the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott pauses over it, and then only to weigh a syllable. "At the saying," he notes, answers to Samuel's "according to," the whole difference being that of "the one tittle" — the small projection of a letter — of which our Lord speaks in Matthew 5:18. And "which he spake in the name of the LORD" stands where Samuel reads "as the LORD commanded"; the variation, he says, is merely verbal.

The single, slender comment is more eloquent than its brevity suggests. Ellicott's whole labour on this chapter has been to set the two accounts side by side and measure their divergences; and here, at the moment of David's obedience, he finds the two texts agreeing so nearly that the only difference is the turn of a single letter. The witness of transmission, in other words, is that the narrative of the king's compliance has come down through both channels essentially unchanged — a quiet testimony to the fidelity with which the sacred record was kept, and an apt place for it, since the verse itself is about a word faithfully received and faithfully done.

For the burden of the verse is the promptness of David's response. No sooner is the command given through Gad than "David went up." The king who had overruled Joab's wise counsel and pressed on with a needless census now moves at once at the prophet's word, asking nothing, delaying nothing. It is the very reversal the earlier witnesses had longed to see in him: the same strong will that once drove headlong into a needless census, prevailing against every remonstrance, now bends as promptly to the plain word of God. Confession in v.8 and self-offering in v.17 here issue in the obedience that always completes true repentance: the penitent does not merely mourn the sin but rises and does the thing God commands. The altar is not yet built, but the feet are already climbing toward it, and the verse leaves David on the way up, obeying the word he has received in the name of the LORD.
20:
"And Ornan turned back, and saw the angel; and his four sons with him hid themselves. Now Ornan was threshing wheat." The verse hides a textual crux, and the witnesses divide over it. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke lays out the evidence: the Septuagint reads that Ornan turned and "saw the king," the Syriac and Arabic say David saw the angel and drop Ornan altogether, and Houbigant, reading hammelech (the king) for hammalach (the angel), defends this from 2 Samuel 24:20, where Araunah "saw David" but nothing is said of his seeing the angel. Houbigant would make this verse Ornan's sight of the king at a distance, prompting the four sons to hide, and v.21 his meeting with David face to face; and Clarke notes that one of Kennicott's manuscripts also reads "the king."

The grammatical-historical witnesses split. Charles Ellicott holds that "there can be little doubt this is corrupt," and that Samuel is right — "Araunah looked up, and saw the king and his servants passing by" — the Hebrew words for "returned" and "looked up," and for "angel" and "king," being easily confused in a faded manuscript. But Keil and Delitzsch defend the received text: "and Ornan turned him about," they say, and Bertheau's rendering "then Ornan turned back" is incorrect and against the context, building upon that error "a whole nest of conjectures." For them Ornan genuinely turned and saw the angel. Both note that "now Ornan was threshing wheat" rings true to the scene, Ellicott adding that the Septuagint appends to 2 Samuel 24:15 the note that "it was the days of wheat harvest."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry takes the harder reading at face value and finds its point: Ornan had himself seen the angel, which so terrified him that he and his four sons hid themselves, unable to bear the brightness of his glory and afraid of his drawn sword; and those who are duly sensible of the terrors of the Lord will do all they can toward reconciliation. So the debate stands: did Ornan look up and see "the angel," with the Masoretic text, Keil, and Henry, or "the king," with the Septuagint, the versions, Houbigant, Kennicott's manuscript, and Ellicott?
21:
"And as David came to Ornan, Ornan looked and saw David, and went out of the threshingfloor, and bowed himself to David with his face to the ground." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, whose comment gathers up the meeting and the bargain of the next verses, present the scene simply as the encounter that sets the purchase in motion: David comes, Ornan sees him, leaves his threshing, and does obeisance. Charles Ellicott notes that this clause — "as David came to Ornan, Ornan looked and saw David" — is wanting in Samuel; the corruption of the previous verse, he observes, "made some such statement necessary here," and the rest of the verse nearly corresponds with 2 Samuel 24:20.

The two grammatical-historical witnesses do not divide, and the plainness of their notes leaves the scene to speak. Ornan is a Jebusite — one of the old Canaanite inhabitants of the very Jerusalem David rules — and here he goes out from his floor and bows to the ground before the king of Israel. It is an act of full and reverent courtesy, the more striking for coming from a man of the conquered people, and the more moving because, as the earlier witnesses noted, he has just seen the terror of the angel and knows the king comes on no ordinary errand.

The quiet significance of the meeting lies in who stands on this ground and what it will become. A penitent king, fresh from offering his own life for his people, comes down to a Gentile's threshing-floor; the Jebusite rises from his wheat to meet him; and the courtesy exchanged between them is the threshold of the whole transaction that follows — the buying of the floor, the building of the altar, and, though neither man yet knows it, the fixing of the site where Israel's temple will one day stand. The bow of Ornan the Jebusite before David is thus a small hinge on which a great history turns, the moment the future holy place passes, by willing sale, from a Canaanite's hand toward the LORD's. Keil and Delitzsch, for their part, simply carry the reader forward from this bow of homage into the bargaining that follows, treating the verse as the quiet hinge between the vision of the angel and the purchase of the ground.
22:
David states his errand: "Grant me the place of this threshingfloor, that I may build an altar therein unto the LORD: thou shalt grant it me for the full price: that the plague may be stayed from the people." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the opening, "Pray give me the place of the threshingfloor, that I may build," and marks how Chronicles compresses Samuel, where Araunah first asks, "Why is my lord the king come to his servant?" and David answers, "To purchase from thee the threshingfloor, to build," and so on. The insistence "at a full price give it me" is not in Samuel; Ellicott hears behind it the memory of Genesis 23:9, Abraham's purchase of the cave of Machpelah, whose narrative "may have caused the modification of the present." Keil and Delitzsch, in the block that carries these verses, give David's words their force: he asks the place "for the full price, that the plague may be stayed from the people."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry fixes on the principle in that phrase: "David immediately made a bargain with Ornan for the threshingfloor; for he would not serve God at other people's charge." Even in the urgency of a raging plague, the king will not take the ground for nothing. Ellicott presses the Machpelah parallel further, suggesting that the very recollection of Abraham's insistence on paying the full worth of a burial-place may have shaped the Chronicler's wording here.

The witnesses agree, and the echo Ellicott catches from Machpelah deepens the moment. As Abraham, offered the burial-cave for a gift, pressed to pay its full worth, so David, at the founding of Israel's place of sacrifice, refuses to build the LORD's altar on land that has cost him nothing. The purpose named — "that the plague may be stayed from the people" — keeps the shepherd's heart of v.17 in view: the altar is sought not as a monument but as the means of the people's deliverance. Yet even that holy urgency does not make David a beggar or a taker; the man who has just confessed his sin will honour God with what is honestly his own, and asks for the floor only on the terms of a fair and full purchase.
23:
Ornan answers with open-handed generosity: "Take it to thee, and let my lord the king do that which is good in his eyes: lo, I give thee the oxen also for burnt offerings, and the threshing instruments for wood, and the wheat for the meat offering; I give it all." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott hears in "Take it to thee" the very courtesy of Genesis 23:11, Ephron's like offer to Abraham. "Let my lord the king do" answers to Samuel's "offer," one letter differing in the Hebrew; "I give thee" is not in Samuel, an exegetical addition; the "threshing instruments," or drags (moraq), is a rare word found besides only in Isaiah 41:15 and 2 Samuel 24:22; and "the wheat for the meat offering," though absent from Samuel, is probably part of the oldest text. He notes that Samuel adds Araunah's blessing, "The LORD thy God accept thee," which the Chronicler here omits. Keil and Delitzsch, in the same block, render the whole of Ornan's lavish offer — the floor, the oxen for burnt-offerings, the threshing-sledges for wood, the wheat for the meal-offering, "I give it all."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads the motive beneath the generosity: Ornan "generously offered it to him gratis, not only in complaisance to the king, but because he had himself seen the angel," and under those apprehensions "was willing to do anything towards making the atonement."

The witnesses converge, and the Machpelah pattern Ellicott traced at the previous verse completes itself here: as Ephron pressed the field and cave upon Abraham as a gift, so Ornan the Jebusite presses floor and oxen and wheat upon David, offering not only the ground but the whole apparatus of sacrifice ready to hand. It is a portrait of a man moved by the terror he has seen to give freely toward the turning away of wrath — a Gentile eager to further the atonement of Israel's God. And it sets the stage for David's refusal in the next verse, for the very lavishness of the offer is what the king's conscience will not accept: an altar must cost its builder something, and Ornan's generosity, however sincere, cannot be allowed to make David's worship cheap.
24:
David refuses the gift outright: "Nay; but I will verily buy it for the full price: for I will not take that which is thine for the LORD, nor offer burnt offerings without cost." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that Chronicles' emphatic "for the full price" answers to Samuel's simpler "at a price," a different word; the following clause, "I will not take that which is thine for the LORD," is not in Samuel but "may well be original"; and the closing refusal, "nor offer burnt offerings without cost," stands in both. On the principle of it he cites Ewald: "it was of the essence of sacrifice to surrender something valued in order to win from God a greater good." Keil and Delitzsch, in the block carrying these verses, give the king's words their full resolve — he will not take Ornan's property for the LORD, nor offer that which costs him nothing.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry compresses the whole into the maxim he had drawn at the bargain: David "would not serve God at other people's charge."

Here the witnesses lay bare a principle that outlives the occasion. Ornan's generosity was real, and in another matter might have been gladly received; but an offering that costs the offerer nothing is, by the very nature of sacrifice, no sacrifice at all. To lay another man's oxen on the altar, or to build on ground one has not paid for, would be to seek the greater good of God's favour without surrendering anything of one's own — the contradiction Ewald names, as reported by Ellicott. David, freshly humbled and freshly forgiven, will have none of it. The atonement for a sin that was peculiarly his own must be made at his own charge; the pardoned king insists on paying, because he understands that worship which risks and relinquishes nothing is worth nothing. So the refusal is not mere courtesy but theology: the altar that will stand on this spot must be founded, from its first stone, on the costly principle that what is offered to God must genuinely be the offerer's to give.
25:
"So David gave to Ornan for the place six hundred shekels of gold by weight" — and the chapter's third and sharpest numerical divergence from Samuel appears, for 2 Samuel 24:24 records only fifty shekels of silver. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note the difference and refer the reader to their fuller discussion on the Samuel passage. Charles Ellicott spreads the problem out: "the two estimates are obviously discordant." Comparing Genesis 23:16, where four hundred shekels of silver buy the field and cave of Machpelah, he judges fifty shekels of silver too little and six hundred shekels of gold far too high for a threshing-floor, and wonders whether "gold" should be read "silver." He weighs the suggestion that the two writers spoke of different things — Samuel pricing only the floor and oxen, the Chronicler "the place" meaning the whole Mount of the Temple, Moriah — but finds that the narratives seem to identify the purchases. Since, as Madden observes, "there is no positive mention of the use of gold money among the Hebrews" apart from this passage, Ellicott reckons ours "probably the later form of the story," and compares a Babylonian contract-tablet recording a house at Borsippa sold for six hundred and ninety shekels of silver under Nabonidus.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley offers the harmonizing reading: David "gave fifty shekels of gold" as Samuel says, "that is, he gave in gold the value of six hundred shekels of silver" — the two figures naming the same sum in different metals.

So the debate is fully joined: fifty shekels of silver in Samuel, six hundred of gold in Chronicles. Wesley reconciles them by exchange, fifty of gold equalling six hundred of silver in worth. Ellicott doubts the harmony can be so cheaply bought, proposing instead a scribal "gold" for "silver," or a difference in what was priced — the bare floor against the whole temple mount — while judging the Chronicler's figure the later tradition. Keil rests on his earlier treatment. What none of them lets slip is the settled fact beneath the disputed price: David paid, and paid dearly, and the ground on which Israel's temple would rise was bought, not seized, at a cost the king felt.
26:
"And David built there an altar unto the LORD, and offered burnt offerings and peace offerings, and called upon the LORD; and he answered him from heaven by fire upon the altar of burnt offering." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note that Chronicles amplifies Samuel's brief "and the LORD was entreated for the land": here David calls upon the LORD, who answers with fire from heaven upon the altar; and the reality of this sign, they insist, is placed beyond doubt by the analogous cases of Leviticus 9:24 and 1 Kings 18:38. Charles Ellicott adds that "and called upon the LORD" is not in Samuel, and reads the fire from heaven as "the Divine inauguration of the new altar and place of sacrifice," a sign David's prayer was heard, comparing again Leviticus 9:24, Elijah's sacrifice at 1 Kings 18:38, and 2 Chronicles 7:1.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke states the plain sense — in answer to David's prayers, God, to show that he had accepted him and was pacified toward him and the people, sent fire from heaven and consumed the offerings. But it is the wesleyan-arminian John Wesley and the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry who, in nearly identical words, draw the gospel from the flame. The fire, says Wesley, "that might justly have fastened on the sinner, fastened upon the sacrifice and consumed it. Thus Christ was made sin and a curse for us, and it pleased the Lord to bruise him, that through him God might be to us, not a consuming fire, but a reconciled Father." Henry, marking that God "answered him from heaven by fire" as the token that his anger was turned away, says the same: the fire that might justly have fallen on the sinner fell on the sacrifice and consumed it, so that Christ was made sin and a curse for us.

The witnesses close ranks around a single wonder. Here two schools that so often part — the wesleyan-arminian and the puritan-evangelical — meet in one confession, that the descending fire is a picture of substitution: judgment falling not on the guilty man but on the offering set in his place. The grammatical-historical voices supply the biblical pattern of such fire, from Sinai's tabernacle to Carmel to Solomon's temple; the evangelical voices supply its meaning; and together they present the answered prayer as both the historical inauguration of a holy site and a shadow of the cross.
27:
"And the LORD commanded the angel; and he put up his sword again into the sheath thereof." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the sheathing as "a figurative expression for the stopping of the pestilence" — the visible drama of the sword returned answering to the unseen cessation of the plague. Charles Ellicott, the other grammatical-historical voice, presses back against reducing the scene too far. It seems to him "hardly fair to call this verse a merely figurative or poetical expression for the cessation of the plague": in v.16 David saw the angel with drawn sword, and the older text of 2 Samuel 24:16-17 equally makes the angel "a real concrete being," not a personification, as Reuss would have it. On the wording he adds a philological touch: "sheath" (nadan) is a word found only here, a very similar term being applied to the body as the sheath of the soul in Daniel 7:15.

The two are not finally opposed — Keil's "figurative" describes the sheathed sword as the sign of the plague's end, while Ellicott's concern is only to resist Reuss's dissolving of the angel into a mere personification. Between them the verse keeps both its truths: the returning of the sword is indeed the outward token that the pestilence is stayed, and yet the being who sheathes it is no poetic figure but a real messenger of God, the same whose drawn blade had made David and his elders fall on their faces.

The image itself is one of completed mercy. The sword that had been stretched out over Jerusalem, that had struck down seventy thousand, that had stood poised at Ornan's floor, is now, at the LORD's command, put back into its sheath. Judgment has run its course and is recalled; the God who "commanded the angel" to smite now commands him to cease. And the philological curiosity Ellicott notes — that the rare word for the sword's sheath is elsewhere the sheath of the soul — quietly hints that behind the staying of this plague stands the God who holds the life of every soul in his hand, to draw or to sheathe as he wills.
28:
"At that time when David saw that the LORD had answered him in the threshingfloor of Ornan the Jebusite, then he sacrificed there." With the plague stayed, the narrative pauses to explain how this floor became a standing place of worship. The reformed John Gill notes first the identity of the man — Ornan is the same as Araunah, "with some small variation of the letters," both names, as Hillerus observes, signifying the ash or pine. In his threshing-floor David now was, where he had prayed and sacrificed, and God had accepted his prayer, as the Targum says, answering by causing fire to fall on the sacrifice and consume it, and by ordering the angel to put up his sword into its sheath.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott draws the consequence: the use of Ornan's floor as a place of sacrifice "was continued from the time of the cessation of the pestilence," and the words "then he sacrificed there" refer to this continuing fact. The answer by fire from heaven of v.26, he says, was "an unmistakable intimation of the Divine will that it should be so," comparing the holy ground of Joshua 5:15. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads the same continuance in the tense: when David perceived that his sacrifice was acceptable to God, "he proceeded to offer more sacrifices in that place."

The witnesses agree, and the verse they expound is the quiet birth of a sanctuary. What had been a Jebusite's working floor, then the scene of a terror and a deliverance, now becomes by divine intimation a place of continuing sacrifice. The fire from heaven did more than answer one prayer; it marked the spot, as surely as the voice at the burning bush marked its ground holy, and David reads the sign aright and keeps returning to worship where God has met him. So the temple's future is already stirring here: the site is chosen not by royal decree but by the descending fire, and David's continued sacrifice at Ornan's floor is the first keeping of the place that will be the house of the LORD.
29:
"For the tabernacle of the LORD, which Moses made in the wilderness, and the altar of the burnt offering, were at that season in the high place at Gibeon." The verse is a parenthesis, and the two witnesses handle it briefly. The reformed John Gill glosses only the making of the tabernacle — that which "Moses made," or rather ordered to be made, by the command of God and according to his direction. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sets the note in its purpose: "the tabernacle" is here named as the recognized dwelling-place of Jehovah, "in contrast with Ornan's threshingfloor, the new sanctuary."

Slight as the comments are, they open the tension the verse exists to register. At this very hour there stood, miles away at Gibeon, the ancient and authorized centre of Israel's worship — the Mosaic tabernacle and the bronze altar of burnt offering, the proper place, by the law's own appointment, at which sacrifice should be made. Yet God has just answered David by fire at a Jebusite's threshing-floor, far from that sanctioned altar. Ellicott's single phrase catches the whole difficulty: the old dwelling-place of the LORD and the new-appointed site are held together for a moment in one sentence, the tabernacle at Gibeon on one side and Ornan's floor on the other.

The Chronicler inserts the note, then, not as idle geography but to justify what might otherwise seem irregular — David's sacrificing away from the lawful altar. The reader is being told, before the reason is given in the next verse, that David knew perfectly well where the tabernacle stood; his worship at Ornan's floor was no forgetful breach of order. The very naming of Gibeon prepares the explanation to come, and quietly marks the moment of transition, when the divinely chosen site of the future temple begins, under the pressure of the plague and the sign of the fire, to eclipse the venerable tent that Moses made. Gill's small insistence that Moses only "made" the tabernacle by the command and direction of God keeps the point that even the old sanctuary was never a human invention but a divine appointment — which is why its momentary eclipse by Ornan's floor could come about only through an equally divine intimation.
30:
The chapter's last verse gives the reason for all that has gone before: "But David could not go before it to inquire of God: for he was afraid because of the sword of the angel of the LORD." The reformed John Gill marks the plain difficulty — David could not go before the tabernacle to inquire of God, "which yet was the proper place to seek the Lord in" — and lets the verse supply its own reason. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke makes the whole point of the note explicit: this is why David built an altar at Ornan's floor at all. Either he was afraid to go to Gibeon because of the destroying angel's sword, or he was afraid of the delay the journey would require, "lest the destroying angel should in the meanwhile exterminate the people"; so he hastily built an altar on the spot, and by the fire from heaven God showed he accepted it — such interventions, Clarke adds, serving to maintain in the people a full persuasion of the truth and divine origin of their religion.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott canvasses the explanations. "David could not go before it" — the tabernacle and altar at Gibeon of 1 Chronicles 16:39 — "to enquire of God," that is, to seek his favour by sacrifice and prayer. Keil, he reports, took it that David could not go to Gibeon "because of the sword of the angel, i.e., on account of the pestilence which raged at Gibeon"; others thought the vision had struck him with some bodily weakness; but Ellicott prefers a more natural reading — the menacing apparition so overawed the king that he durst not follow the usual course, the sight making an indelible impression as to the sanctity of the place where it appeared, as at Genesis 28:17 and Exodus 3:5. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley adds the fear for the city: seeing the angel with drawn sword over Jerusalem, David durst not go off to Gibeon "lest the angel in the mean time should destroy Jerusalem," and so worshipped in the place God had consecrated by his special presence and acceptance.

So the witnesses close the chapter debating why David stayed — the plague raging at Gibeon (Keil, as Ellicott reports), the peril of leaving the poised angel over Jerusalem (Clarke and Wesley), or the sheer awe of the apparition and the new sense of the holiness of the spot (Ellicott). The differences are of emphasis, not substance, for all agree on the result: David worshipped where God had met him, and the Jebusite's threshing-floor, sealed by fire and chosen by the sword's alarm, became the ground of the house of the LORD.
